title: Catching Errors 🧤
description: Use the do-catch block to peacefully resolve a thrown error.

slides.title: The Catcher's Mitt 🧤
slides.content:
# Handling the Throw

If a dangerous function throws an error like a baseball, you must be ready to catch it! 

We do this using a `do { } catch { }` block.

Inside the `do` block, we attempt to run the risky function. We MUST explicitly place the `try` keyword right before it.

slides.title: The Syntax

quizData.question: If you call a function that is marked with `throws`, what keyword MUST you place in front of it when you call it?
quizData.explanation: Swift forces you to type `try` (e.g., `try myFunction()`) so that when you are reading the code 6 months later, you instantly know 'Oh, this is a dangerous function call that could fail!'

quizData.question: What happens to your App if a dangerous function executes a `throw`, but you forgot to put it inside a `do-catch` block?

options.text: Swift throws a compiler error and firmly refuses to build the app.

quizData.explanation: Swift's primary goal is safety. It simply will not allow you to compile an app that throws errors wildly without catching them.

slides.title: See It in Action
slides.content: Change the password to '1234' and watch the code completely skip the `catch` block and succeed!
